You'll also participate in an on-call rotation for after-hours emergencies. That's a real commitment, and it's worth knowing going in. It's not every night, but when it's your week, it's your week.

  • Complete resident maintenance service requests across all major trades
  • Perform apartment turnovers and make-ready work
  • Handle appliance repairs, painting, and general upkeep
  • Maintain grounds, offices, and common areas
  • Participate in on-call emergency rotation

What MG Properties Is Looking For

Two or more years of hands-on apartment maintenance experience is preferred, ideally on a property of 200 units or larger. That scale matters because larger communities generate more concurrent work orders, and you need to be able to triage and prioritize without constant direction. Multi-trade competency is required, not optional. Customer service attitude is listed as a key qualifier, and they mean it. Residents interact with maintenance staff more directly than almost anyone else on a property team, and how you show up at someone's door sets the tone for how they feel about where they live.

Reliable transportation is required. You'll need to get yourself to the property and, depending on the on-call situation, potentially respond from home.

Compensation and Team Culture

The pay range is $25 to $26 per hour depending on experience, with monthly bonuses and company profit sharing on top of base wages. The benefits package includes medical, dental, and vision coverage, a 401(k) with employer match, paid time off, paid holidays, paid sick time, and rental discounts. That last one is meaningful if you're local to the Vallejo area and looking to reduce your own housing costs.

MG Properties talks about employee development as a real priority, not just a line in a job description. For a technician with broad trade skills, that can translate into a path toward lead maintenance or even a maintenance supervisor role as the company continues to grow its Western U.S. portfolio. The skills you build managing turns, handling complex service requests, and working across trades are directly transferable to higher-level maintenance leadership positions. Companies like this tend to promote from within when they find people who show up consistently and communicate well with both residents and management.

If you've got solid hands-on experience, a calm approach to resident interactions, and you're comfortable being part of an on-call rotation, this is a role worth taking seriously.

job.title: Dual-Site Leasing & Marketing Manager
job.description:
Scion is one of the more established names in student housing, and this role sits at the intersection of leasing, marketing, and community operations across two Fort Collins properties. The title is Customer Experience Manager, but the job is really about driving occupancy and retention while keeping two communities running smoothly day to day.

You'll split your time between both sites, which means your organizational discipline matters as much as your sales instincts. Student housing has its own rhythm. Traffic surges during lease-up season, the annual turn is its own project within the job, and your residents are signing leases months before they move in. You need to understand that cycle and stay ahead of it.

On the leasing and marketing side, you're executing social media strategy across Instagram, TikTok, and Facebook, running on-campus events, doing weekly market surveys, and working toward revenue goals tracked through KPIs. You're not just reporting numbers. You're expected to read trends and adjust. On the operations side, you're inspecting curb appeal, coordinating work orders, keeping tabs on resident satisfaction, and helping plan and execute the annual turnover process alongside the General Manager and Facilities Supervisor. Student housing turns are high-volume and time-compressed. If you've done one, you know what that means.

This is also a people leadership role. You'll coach Experience Team Members, help with onboarding, and make sure the team stays Fair Housing compliant. You won't be managing the property solo. You're supporting the GM, but you're also expected to lead independently when needed.

What this role requires

  • At least two years in customer service, sales, or community management (student housing experience is a real advantage here)
  • Comfort working up to six days per week during peak seasons, including turn
  • Proficiency in Microsoft Office and ideally Entrata
  • Strong written and verbal communication across formats: in-person, social, email
  • Ability to manage competing priorities across two sites without dropping details
  • College degree preferred but not required with relevant experience

The base salary range is $45,000 to $55,000, with a discretionary annual bonus and a full benefits package including health, dental, vision, 401k matching, and paid parental leave. Visa sponsorship is not available for this position.

One thing worth knowing: candidates who perform well in student housing CXM roles tend to move into General Manager positions relatively quickly. The dual-site scope here gives you exposure that single-property roles don't, and Scion's size means there are paths forward if you perform. That's not a guarantee, but it's a real pattern in this sector.

job.title: Insurance Claims Administrator
job.description:
A vendor's certificate of insurance expired three weeks ago. Nobody caught it. Now there's a slip-and-fall claim at one of the properties, and suddenly everyone's looking for the file. That's the kind of situation this role exists to prevent, and to clean up when prevention fails.

What This Role Actually Covers

The Scion Group operates in the student housing space, and their Chicago corporate office is looking for an Insurance Claims Administrator to sit within the legal and compliance function. It's a broad seat. You're not just tracking claims, you're also helping draft and revise housing agreements and commercial contracts, supporting outside counsel on litigation matters, and keeping vendor insurance compliance from falling through the cracks.

Day to day, you'll coordinate the full lifecycle of insurance claims on behalf of the company and its managed properties, respond to property staff questions about legal and policy matters, and flag resident complaints or regulatory issues that need attorney attention. You'll also support entity and asset acquisition work by coordinating with inside and outside counsel. The role reports into the legal team, with direction coming from staff attorneys, the VP of Legal Affairs, and General Counsel.

This is a corporate headquarters position with standard business hours. No on-call, no weekends managing turns. That's a real differentiator from most property-side roles.

What They're Looking For

  • Associate's degree or paralegal certification preferred
  • At least one year in a legal, insurance, or administrative role (a plus, not a hard requirement)
  • Solid organizational skills and clear written communication
  • Comfort with Microsoft Office; familiarity with property management systems is a bonus
  • Eligibility to work in the U.S. (no visa sponsorship available)

The salary range is $50,000 to $65,000 base, plus a discretionary annual bonus. Benefits include health, dental, and vision coverage, 401k matching, paid parental and maternal leave, and learning reimbursement.

What separates the candidates who thrive in this kind of role from those who struggle is usually not legal knowledge. It's the ability to track a dozen open items simultaneously without letting anything go quiet. Insurance claims have a way of aging badly when nobody's watching them. If you're the person who builds the spreadsheet before anyone asks you to, you'll fit right in.

For someone with paralegal ambitions or an interest in real estate law, this is a legitimate foot in the door at a company with a significant portfolio footprint in the student housing sector.

job.title: Leasing Consultant
job.description:
A prospective resident stops by Saturday afternoon with her parents in tow, a stack of questions, and a move-in date that's six weeks out. You're the first real impression Cottages of Clemson makes on all three of them. How that conversation goes matters more than almost anything else happening that day.

What This Role Actually Covers

The Experience Team Member title is a deliberate one. Scion builds its student communities around the idea that leasing and customer experience aren't separate functions, and this role reflects that. You're doing tours and closing leads, yes. But you're also helping plan and execute resident engagement events, managing the social media presence for the community, fielding package pickups, and occasionally handling after-hours lockout calls when you're on rotation. The job is genuinely varied, and some days will pull you in four directions at once.

On the administrative side, you'll track maintenance requests and follow up to make sure they move forward, keep the office and common areas presentable, and stay current on community policies, floor plans, and local market knowledge so you can answer questions with confidence rather than guessing. You'll also pass feedback up to management when you're hearing the same objections or concerns from multiple prospects. That kind of ground-level insight is useful, and Scion actually wants it.

Student housing has its own rhythm. The leasing cycle is compressed, turn season is intense, and your resident population turns over in large waves. If you've worked in conventional multifamily, the pace here is different. If you're coming in fresh, expect the summer and turn periods to be genuinely demanding stretches that require full availability.

What Scion Is Looking For

  • Strong interpersonal skills with a wide range of people, including residents, parents, and university contacts
  • Comfort with an unpredictable daily schedule and a willingness to shift priorities quickly
  • Reliability. This is a team environment, and your coworkers depend on you showing up and following through
  • Availability during summers, holidays, and turn periods, which are non-negotiable busy seasons in student housing
  • Eligibility to work in the United States (visa sponsorship is not available)

What separates strong candidates here isn't a leasing background, it's genuine people instincts combined with the discipline to handle the administrative side without letting it slip. Plenty of personable candidates struggle with the follow-through. The ones who do well in student leasing tend to be organized enough to track details while staying warm and present in every conversation.

This role is based on-site at Cottages of Clemson in Central, South Carolina, with standard business hours plus an after-hours on-call rotation shared among the team.

job.title: Leasing Consultant
job.description:
Student housing leasing runs on a cycle unlike any other asset class. You're not managing steady monthly traffic; you're building toward a single renewal and new lease deadline that determines whether the property hits occupancy before the academic year starts. At Alight Fullerton, that cycle is constant, and the Experience Team Member role sits right at the center of it.

Scion is one of the larger purpose-built student housing operators in the country, and this part-time position at their Fullerton property is a ground-level leasing and customer experience role. The title is Experience Team Member, but the work is real leasing work: tours, follow-up, sales conversations, handling objections, and feeding intel back to the management team about what's stalling conversions. You're also the face of the community on social platforms and at resident events, which matters in student housing where peer referrals and Instagram presence actually move traffic.

What the Day-to-Day Actually Looks Like

On a typical shift, you might open the office, walk the clubhouse and amenity spaces to make sure they're presentable, and then run back-to-back tours with prospective residents or their parents. Package logging, maintenance request coordination, and leasing admin fill the gaps. There's an after-hours on-call rotation for lockouts, so flexibility isn't just a preference; it's a real requirement. The role ramps up hard during Turn, the period between leases when units flip for incoming residents, and availability during summers and holidays is expected.

  • Conduct property and amenity tours for prospective residents
  • Execute leasing and engagement initiatives set by the management team
  • Support social media content and community outreach
  • Log and retrieve resident packages
  • Coordinate maintenance request follow-up
  • Assist with office and common area upkeep
  • Participate in after-hours lockout rotation as needed

Who Tends to Do Well Here

Candidates who thrive in this role usually have one thing in common: they're genuinely comfortable talking to a wide range of people, from 18-year-old freshmen to skeptical parents co-signing a lease. Strong performers don't wait for traffic to come to them; they're proactive about local marketing ideas and notice when a sales objection keeps coming up, then flag it. Average candidates show up for tours and call it done. Strong ones treat every interaction as a data point.

This is a part-time role paying $17 to $19 per hour in California. It's a solid entry point into property management, and student housing experience specifically transfers well to conventional multifamily leasing roles down the road, particularly for anyone interested in eventually moving into an Assistant Manager or Leasing Manager position at a larger community.

job.title: Assistant Community Manager
job.description:
Hawthorne Residential Partners operates around 50 communities across the Southeast, and Park Forest in Greensboro is one of those properties. The Assistant Community Manager role here is genuinely a two-track job: part leasing, part finance, part team leadership when the Community Manager is out. You're not just answering phones and giving tours. You're watching delinquency reports, posting payments, reviewing ledgers, and making sure the numbers tell an accurate story before they hit regional leadership.

Greensboro's rental market has stayed competitive, with steady renter demand driven in part by the university presence and a consistent in-migration of workforce renters. That context matters because occupancy and renewals aren't passive here. You'll need to actively convert traffic and retain residents through genuine relationship-building, not just lease incentives.

Day to day, this role moves fast. Mornings might involve delinquency follow-up calls and ledger audits. Afternoons shift toward tours, lease paperwork, and renewal conversations. Saturdays are working days, and the office runs Monday through Friday from 9 to 6, with Saturdays from 10 to 4. One weekday off rotates in to keep the schedule at 40 hours. Resident events can pull you in on evenings too, so flexibility isn't optional.

What separates a strong candidate here from an average one is comfort with the financial side of operations. A lot of people come into assistant manager roles with solid leasing instincts but shaky confidence on rent collection, eviction coordination, and reading a financial report with real understanding. If you can hold both skill sets at once, you'll move faster through Hawthorne's internal Career Path Program, which is a real pathway toward Community Manager, not just language in a job posting.

What Hawthorne is Looking For

  • Two years of property management experience, ideally with at least six months in an assistant manager seat
  • Demonstrated ability to handle delinquency management, payment posting, and ledger review
  • Comfort leading a small team when the Community Manager is unavailable
  • Strong conflict resolution skills, especially in resident-facing situations that require patience and follow-through
  • Valid driver's license; a CALP designation is a bonus but not required
  • High school diploma or GED equivalent

Compensation includes an hourly base plus monthly leasing and renewal commissions and quarterly performance bonuses. Benefits cover medical, dental, vision, 401k with match, paid time off including your birthday, pet insurance, telehealth access, and paid parental and adoption leave. Life insurance is company-paid.

If you've been doing leasing work and want to grow into a role with real financial responsibility and a clear path toward community management, this position is structured to support that move.

job.title: Assistant Community Manager
job.description:
What You'll Actually Do

This role sits at the intersection of leasing, finance, and team operations. On any given day, you might open the office, pull a delinquency report, follow up on outstanding balances by phone, then shift immediately into conducting a tour for a prospect who walked in off the street. That range is real, and it's daily.

You'll support leasing activity end to end: responding to leads, walking prospects through available homes, and making sure lease applications and renewals are completed accurately and on time. Occupancy goals are shared goals here, and your leasing performance contributes directly to them. You'll also manage the community's social media presence, which means you're thinking about resident engagement and community identity, not just transaction volume.

On the financial side, you're handling rent collection, payment posting, ledger reviews, and delinquency follow-up. When accounts go sideways, you're coordinating the eviction process alongside the Community Manager. This isn't background administrative work. You're expected to understand what the numbers mean and flag problems before they compound.

When the Community Manager is out, you run the operation. That means keeping the team on task, handling resident concerns with professionalism, and making judgment calls that keep things moving. The people who thrive here don't wait to be told what needs attention.

What You Bring

  • At least two years of property management experience, with six months in an assistant manager capacity preferred
  • Comfort reading and interpreting financial reports, including delinquency tracking and account ledgers
  • Leasing experience: you know how to convert a tour, not just conduct one
  • Strong conflict resolution instincts. Resident disputes happen. How you handle them matters.
  • A valid driver's license
  • CALP accreditation is a plus but not required

The Bigger Picture

Hawthorne Residential Partners is a top-50 multifamily management company with deep roots in the Southeast. Atlanta is a competitive multifamily market with consistent renter demand and a wide spectrum of properties across Class A and B assets. Knowing how to drive retention in that environment, not just traffic, is what separates strong assistant managers from ones who plateau.

Hawthorne has a formal Career Path Program that connects this role directly to a Community Manager seat. If you're someone who wants to run your own community within a defined timeline, this structure exists to make that happen. Total targeted compensation is $75,000 including hourly pay, monthly leasing and renewal commissions, and quarterly performance bonuses. Benefits include medical, dental, vision, 401k match, paid parental leave, and paid time off that includes your birthday.

The schedule runs Monday through Friday with Saturday hours required. One weekday off is built in to keep you at 40 hours. Resident events may occasionally pull you outside standard hours. That's the honest reality of community management, and it's worth knowing before you apply.

job.title: Community Manager
job.description:
It's a Tuesday morning and you're already fielding a call from a frustrated resident before the leasing office opens, reviewing a delinquency report, and mentally prepping your team for a busy midweek traffic push. That's the reality of this role. It's demanding, varied, and genuinely rewarding when your community is running the way it should.

Hawthorne Residential Partners, one of the top 50 multifamily management companies in the country and a company with deep Southeast roots, is hiring a Community Manager for one of their Atlanta apartment communities. This is a full-scope operations role. You're responsible for financial performance, leasing results, resident satisfaction, team development, and the day-to-day health of the property. If you've worked your way up through leasing and assistant management and you're ready to own a community, this is worth a close look.

What You're Actually Responsible For

On the financial side, you'll manage the budget, oversee rent collection and deposit accounting, track delinquency, and report on community performance using Yardi. You'll work with ownership and regional leadership, so comfort reading financial reports and making decisions within budget constraints is genuinely necessary, not just a box on the job description.

Operationally, you'll drive leasing and renewal performance to hit occupancy goals, partner with maintenance on work orders and preventative care, manage vendor relationships, and conduct regular property inspections. You'll also handle escalated resident concerns, monitor online reputation, coordinate resident events, and manage social media content for the community.

Team leadership is central to this position. You'll hire, train, and coach both office and maintenance staff, run weekly team meetings, and build a culture of accountability and service. Hawthorne calls it their "Live It" culture, and they expect their Community Managers to model it daily.

What the Right Candidate Looks Like

  • At least two years of property management experience, including time as a Leasing Consultant and Assistant Community Manager
  • Six months of direct Community Manager experience in multifamily, strongly preferred
  • Proficiency in Yardi and Knock preferred; Microsoft Office required
  • Valid driver's license required; CAM, CAPS, or a real estate license is a plus
  • Strong financial literacy, specifically the ability to read and act on reporting rather than just produce it
  • Genuine conflict resolution skills, the kind you've actually used with difficult residents, not just claimed on a resume

The work schedule runs Monday through Friday, 9 to 6, and Saturdays 10 to 4, with one weekday off to maintain a 40-hour week. Some evenings and weekend availability may be needed for resident events.

Atlanta's multifamily market has seen meaningful rent moderation over the past year after a heavy wave of new supply, particularly in submarkets like Midtown, Buckhead, and the northern suburbs. That means Community Managers here are being asked to work harder on retention, get creative with renewals, and keep a close eye on concessions. A candidate who understands how to hold occupancy in a competitive environment without sacrificing NOI is exactly who thrives in this market right now.

Compensation targets $85,000 total, with monthly leasing and renewal commissions and quarterly performance bonuses layered on top of base salary. Benefits include medical, dental, and vision coverage, a 401k match, paid time off including your birthday, pet insurance, and paid parental leave. Hawthorne also runs a formal career path program, and the Community Manager to Regional Manager track is one they actively support.

job.title: Leasing Consultant
job.description:
What You'll Actually Do at Brookefield

This role puts you at the front door, literally and figuratively. You're the first person a prospective resident meets, the voice that answers the phone, and the team member who follows up after a tour to keep a lead warm. Day to day, you're conducting personalized apartment tours, responding to inquiries across phone, email, and web platforms, processing lease applications and move-in paperwork, and helping push the community toward its occupancy and renewal goals.

It's not just transactional work. Hawthorne calls this role a Leasing and Live It Specialist, which signals something real about how they operate. You're expected to build genuine connections with residents, coordinate community events, and help maintain the kind of environment that makes people want to renew. That means you'll be checking in on common areas, supporting social media and local outreach efforts, and keeping tabs on what competitor properties in the Raleigh market are doing.

The schedule is Monday through Friday, 9 to 6, with Saturdays from 10 to 4. You'll get one weekday off to keep the week at 40 hours. Resident events and community needs can occasionally pull you outside those hours, so flexibility matters here.

What You Bring

  • A high school diploma or GED. That's the floor.
  • At least one year in customer service, sales, or apartment leasing is preferred, though not required if you can demonstrate the right instincts
  • A valid driver's license
  • Confidence communicating in person, by phone, and in writing
  • A natural ability to listen to what a prospect actually needs and match them to the right home, not just the next available unit
  • A CALP (Certified Apartment Leasing Professional) credential is a bonus, not a barrier to entry

Strong candidates in leasing roles tend to share one trait that's hard to teach: they're comfortable with the follow-up. Sending the second email, making the check-in call, staying engaged with a lead who went quiet. That persistence, when paired with genuine warmth, is what separates good leasing consultants from great ones.

Where This Role Fits

Hawthorne is a top-50 multifamily operator with deep roots in the Southeast, and Brookefield at 2400 Cluette Drive sits in a well-established Raleigh submarket with real competition for residents. Your market awareness matters here, not just your personality.

On the career side, Hawthorne runs a formal Career Path Program with the specific goal of moving Leasing Consultants into Assistant Community Manager roles. If you want to grow into operations, financial reporting, and team oversight, the path exists and the company invests in it. The leasing skills you build here, objection handling, resident communication, traffic conversion, transfer cleanly into every other role in property management.

Compensation includes an hourly base plus monthly leasing and renewal commissions, along with incentive programs tied to performance. Benefits include medical, dental, vision, 401k match, paid parental and adoption leave, pet insurance, and paid time off that includes your birthday.

job.title: Leasing Consultant
job.description:
Leasing is a skills game. The people who do it well aren't just friendly. They read a prospect quickly, adjust their pitch without being manipulative, handle a "I need to think about it" without desperation, and still find time to follow up on six other leads before lunch. That's the core of what Hawthorne Residential Partners is hiring for at their Atlanta community.

The Skills This Role Runs On

Every day, you're using a short stack of skills repeatedly: active listening, objection handling, written communication, and basic sales discipline. Conducting personalized tours is less about memorizing floor plans and more about figuring out what matters to the person standing in front of you. Is it the commute? The closet space? The dog-friendliness? You ask, you listen, and you connect the dots out loud for them.

Beyond tours, you're processing leasing applications, completing move-in paperwork accurately, and tracking your own follow-up pipeline. The paperwork isn't glamorous, but a missed step during move-in creates problems that ripple into the resident's entire tenure. Precision matters here, not just personality.

You'll also help coordinate resident events and community engagement efforts. These aren't optional extras. They're how a property builds the kind of retention that keeps occupancy healthy without relying entirely on new traffic and concessions.

What Separates a Good Leasing Consultant from a Great One

The honest answer is follow-up discipline. Most prospects don't lease on the first visit. The consultants who convert at a high rate are the ones with a consistent, non-annoying system for staying in touch after a tour. They're also the ones who actually know the competing properties in the submarket, because a prospect asking "how does this compare to the place down the street?" deserves a real answer, not a deflection.

One more thing worth knowing: this schedule includes Saturdays, and resident events can occasionally push into evenings. That's the nature of leasing. If your weekends are completely off-limits, this role will create friction. If you're flexible and like the trade-off of a weekday off during the week, it tends to work well.

Building a Career Here

Hawthorne has a formal career path program, and the Leasing Consultant role is a recognized entry point into property management leadership. The natural next step is Assistant Community Manager, where the skills shift toward operations, financials, and team coordination. The leasing experience you build here, particularly around occupancy management and resident relations, translates directly into that next level.

  • High school diploma or GED required; one year of customer service or sales experience preferred
  • Valid driver's license required
  • CALP certification is a plus but not required on day one
  • Total target compensation around $55,000, including hourly pay plus monthly leasing and renewal commissions
  • Benefits include medical, dental, vision, 401k match, paid time off, pet insurance, and paid parental leave

Hawthorne is one of the larger Southeast-rooted multifamily operators in the country. If you want a company with enough structure to support your growth but enough regional identity to actually feel like something, that combination is harder to find than it sounds.

job.title: Leasing Consultant- Multi Site
job.description:
Multi-site leasing roles aren't for everyone. You're covering more than one community, which means your schedule shifts, your product knowledge has to be sharp across multiple floor plans and rent ranges, and your follow-up game has to be tighter than average. If that sounds like a grind, it probably is. If it sounds like a challenge worth taking on, keep reading.

Hawthorne Residential Partners is one of the larger Southeast-based operators in the country, consistently ranking among the top 50 multifamily management companies nationally. This position is based at Retreat at Knoxville in the 37932 zip code, a growing corridor on the west side of the city that has seen consistent rental demand. The role itself is a leasing consultant position, but because it spans multiple sites, you're essentially running a tighter version of the full leasing cycle on repeat: handle traffic, tour prospects, convert leads, process paperwork, support renewals, and then do it again at the next property.

Day to day, you're the first person a prospective resident talks to, whether that's a walk-in, a phone call, or an email inquiry from an ILS. You're conducting tours, managing follow-up, and building enough rapport that someone chooses your community over the three others they toured that week. You'll also help coordinate resident events and keep an eye on what competitors are doing in the local market. It's a sales role with a service wrapper around it, and the best people in these seats understand that difference instinctively.

What actually separates strong candidates from average ones here isn't just personality. It's consistency. Anyone can have a great tour on a good day. The people who move up in leasing careers are the ones who follow up on the cold leads, who close on a Saturday afternoon when foot traffic has slowed, and who treat the 30th inquiry of the week with the same energy as the first. Hawthorne's own career path program recognizes this: leasing consultant to assistant community manager is a well-worn track in this industry, and operators like Hawthorne tend to promote from within when they see someone who can perform under volume.

What You'll Need

  • High school diploma or GED
  • At least one year in customer service, sales, or apartment leasing (preferred, not required)
  • Valid driver's license (required given the multi-site nature of this role)
  • CALP certification is a plus if you have it
  • Comfortable working Saturdays and occasional evenings for resident events

Compensation includes an hourly base plus monthly leasing and renewal commissions, which is standard for the role. Benefits include medical, dental, vision, 401k with match, paid time off, pet insurance, and paid parental leave options. The office runs Monday through Friday, 9 to 6, and Saturdays, 10 to 4, with one weekday off each week to keep the schedule at 40 hours.

If you're early in your property management career and want to build leasing fundamentals fast, multi-site exposure does that more quickly than a single-property seat. You'll see different resident profiles, different pricing strategies, and more situations in a shorter period of time. That experience compounds.

job.title: Maintenance Supervisor - Multi-Site
job.description:
Multi-site maintenance supervision is a different animal than running a single property. You're not just fixing things. You're managing schedules across communities, tracking vendor timelines on multiple make-readies simultaneously, keeping your team accountable when you're not physically on-site, and still finding time to put hands on equipment when the situation calls for it. Hawthorne Residential Partners is hiring for exactly that kind of role in Atlanta.

The day-to-day here spans the full maintenance lifecycle. Unit turns need to be inspected and signed off before move-in, preventative maintenance programs need to run on a quarterly cadence, and capital improvement projects need bids sourced, schedules built, and execution managed. On any given week you might be walking a punch list on a vacant unit in the morning, meeting with a vendor about an ongoing capex project at noon, and coaching a technician through a proper HVAC repair in the afternoon. The variety is real, and so is the accountability.

Budget monitoring and inventory management fall under this role too. That means you need to understand what things cost, catch waste before it compounds, and make sure your team isn't sitting idle waiting on parts that should have been stocked. Grounds, amenities, and common areas also stay on your radar because curb appeal directly affects traffic and, ultimately, occupancy.

What separates a strong candidate here isn't just technical range, though you do need solid working knowledge across HVAC, plumbing, and electrical. It's the ability to keep multiple communities moving without letting any single one slip. Supervisors who thrive in multi-site roles tend to build tight vendor relationships early, because when a turn is sitting incomplete, a reliable contractor you can call on short notice is worth more than almost anything else.

On-call rotation is part of the job. After-hours emergencies happen, and Hawthorne expects you to respond. That's worth knowing going in.

What Hawthorne Is Looking For

  • High school diploma or GED
  • At least 3 years in apartment maintenance or a skilled trade (HVAC, plumbing, electrical)
  • Prior supervisory or lead tech experience strongly preferred
  • Valid driver's license required
  • EPA certification required
  • CPO certification highly preferred (required in some states)

Compensation includes hourly pay plus a monthly renewal bonus, quarterly performance bonuses, and quarterly resident satisfaction bonuses. Hawthorne also offers a career path program with genuine development structure, education reimbursement for maintenance skills, and a benefits package that includes medical, dental, vision, 401k match, paid parental leave, and pet insurance, among other things.

If you've been running maintenance at a single property and want the complexity and career growth that comes with multi-site responsibility, this is a logical next step. Multi-site supervisors who build strong operational track records often move into regional or director-level maintenance roles, and Hawthorne's internal development program is structured to support that trajectory.

job.title: Regional Property Manager
job.description:
This role lives or dies on two skills: reading a financial statement fast and reading people even faster. The Regional Property Manager position at Hillpointe asks you to do both simultaneously, across a Sun Belt portfolio of workforce housing communities in and around Atlanta.

What This Role Actually Demands

Hillpointe isn't a third-party fee management shop. They develop, build, and operate their own product. That matters because when you're the Regional here, you're not translating an owner's vision through layers of bureaucracy. You're sitting close to the source. Budget variance conversations happen with people who built the asset. That's a different dynamic than most regionals experience.

The financial side is real. You're building annual budgets, managing pricing and renewal strategies inside Entrata, and digging into performance gaps before they show up as NOI problems on the T-12. If you can't spot a delinquency trend early or explain why T-3 occupancy is trending down before your owner asks, this isn't the right seat for you.

But the job posting says "Sales, Sales, Sales" three times, and that's not an accident. Workforce housing in the Sun Belt is competitive. You're competing on price, reputation, and leasing execution. A regional who can only manage expenses but can't drive traffic and close occupancy gaps is only doing half the job. The expectation here is that you build teams who sell, and you model that culture from the top down.

The People Side of the Portfolio

Identifying talent and developing site-level teams is where the leverage is in this role. You can have the best pricing strategy in the market and still lose ground if your on-site managers aren't executing. Hillpointe wants someone who can hire right, train with purpose, and hold teams accountable to leasing goals and deadlines without micromanaging every punch list item.

Regular travel across the portfolio is expected. Site inspections, team check-ins, market surveys. If you're the type who prefers to manage by spreadsheet from a home office, this one isn't for you.

What Separates Strong Candidates Here

  • Entrata fluency, or at minimum, the ability to get up to speed fast on a new platform without hand-holding
  • Proven experience managing lease-up or stabilization across multiple communities at once
  • A real track record in workforce or Class B/C garden-style product, not just Class A high-rise
  • The ability to develop marketing plans that actually move the needle on online leads and tours, not just repost listings
  • A real estate license, or a clear path to obtaining one in the Georgia market
  • Comfort communicating with stakeholders across legal, financial, and HR contexts

Regionals who come up through the ranks at integrated developer-operators tend to have an edge here. They understand that every operational decision connects back to asset value, and they don't need to be reminded of it. If your background includes ownership-side thinking alongside your management experience, that's the profile Hillpointe is building toward.

job.title: Customer Experience Specialist (Leasing/Customer Service)
job.description:
Leasing roles get mislabeled constantly. This one sits at the intersection of sales, customer service, and light operations, and Cottonwood Residential is calling it what it actually is: a customer experience position. That framing matters, because if you think this job is just showing apartments and collecting applications, you'll underperform here.

Park Avenue Apartments in Salt Lake City needs someone who treats every interaction, whether it's a prospect walking in cold, a resident calling about a package, or a vendor dropping off supplies, as worth full attention. You'll be the first voice people hear on the phone and the first face they see at the door. That's not a small thing. First impressions in leasing move the needle on traffic conversion more than most people realize.

Day to day, you're keeping vacant units show-ready, walking the tour path, staying sharp on current pricing and availability, and responding to inquiries across phone, email, and text without letting anything fall through the cracks. You're also helping coordinate community events and supporting social media marketing efforts, which is increasingly part of the leasing consultant role whether it's listed in the job title or not. Package room organization is in the mix too. Not glamorous, but residents notice when it's a mess.

Cottonwood was early to self-guided tours, and that's shaped how they think about the customer journey. They're not looking for someone who just unlocks a door and steps aside. They want someone who can design an experience even when they're not physically in the room.

The schedule is full-time and includes weekends and evenings. That's the job. Leasing doesn't close when the office does, and the candidates who thrive here are the ones who don't resent that reality.

What strong candidates bring

  • Genuine comfort with phone sales and the ability to convert inquiries, not just answer questions
  • Attention to detail on unit readiness and the move-in experience, because a botched make-ready kills a first impression fast
  • Composure when a frustrated resident walks in and the leasing office is the closest target
  • Curiosity about the industry. This role has a stated path toward management, and Cottonwood seems to mean it
  • Comfort with digital communication across multiple channels simultaneously

If you're early in your property management career and want a role that builds real leasing fundamentals at a company that's been willing to rethink how the customer experience works, this is a reasonable place to start. The quarterly bonus structure means your effort has a direct return, which is how it should work in leasing.

job.title: Sales & Resident Experience Consultant
job.description:
What You'll Actually Do

This role sits at the front door of Berkshire's Villages of Briggs Ranch community. You're the first person a prospective resident talks to, and often the reason they sign or walk. Your day moves between leasing calls, in-person tours, follow-up outreach, and keeping your model units and tour routes in presentable shape. The expectation is a closing ratio of at least 20 percent, which is a real number you'll be measured against, not a vague "goal."

Beyond the sales piece, you're also a point of contact for current residents. That means professional communication matters as much as your pitch. You'll track what's happening in the local market, flag trends to your property manager, and bring ideas for improving how the community markets itself. The administrative side is real too. Lease files, follow-up logs, and accurate records don't maintain themselves.

One honest note: leasing is a role where your energy directly affects your results. Slow days still require the same enthusiasm as busy ones, and prospective residents can tell the difference. If you find genuine satisfaction in helping someone find a home, that comes through. If you're just going through the motions, that comes through too.

What You Bring

  • At least two years of customer-facing experience, in property management or a transferable field like retail, hospitality, or sales
  • Comfort with technology and the ability to pick up new software without hand-holding
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills. You'll be writing emails and talking to prospects in the same afternoon
  • An eye for detail when walking tour routes and models. Burnt-out bulbs and scuffed walls matter more than you'd think
  • Genuine interest in market conditions and how your community competes locally

Context Worth Knowing

Berkshire Group owns and manages its own properties, which means the leasing team here isn't working for a third-party operator. Decisions move faster and there's more accountability at the site level. That's a good thing if you want to see the results of your work. It also means your closing numbers and resident satisfaction scores get real attention from leadership.

For someone early in a property management career, this kind of leasing role builds a foundation that transfers well. Strong leasing consultants tend to move into assistant manager roles where they take on delinquency tracking, move-in coordination, and eventually NOI conversations. The skills you build here, reading traffic patterns, working objections, keeping prospects warm through a lease-up cycle, carry weight at every level of the business.

Berkshire offers a 25 percent rent discount, three weeks of vacation, and personal development plans alongside standard benefits. The benefits package is mentioned in the original posting, but the specifics are worth confirming during your interview.

job.title: Leasing Hub Associate - Sandy Spring, GA
job.description:
This isn't a traditional leasing consultant role. No property tours. No move-in paperwork. No resident calls about maintenance. The Leasing Hub Associate at Cortland sits in a centralized hub and works leads remotely, connecting prospective residents to the right communities across Cortland's portfolio before they ever set foot on a property.

The core of the job is volume and conversation. You're fielding and initiating 40 to 50 lead contacts per day through calls, emails, and texts sourced from RealPage. Your job is to qualify those prospects, understand what they actually need in an apartment home, and hand off warm, scheduled appointments to the on-site leasing teams. You're not closing leases yourself. You're filling the top of the funnel and making sure the on-site teams aren't working cold traffic.

That distinction matters. Strong candidates in this role tend to come from inside sales or high-volume customer service backgrounds where consistent outreach is the job, not the exception. If you're used to reactive service work and waiting for inbound volume, the daily outbound pace here will feel like a shift. People who thrive are the ones who treat every follow-up as a real sales touchpoint, not just a task to check off.

You'll need a genuine command of Cortland's communities, pricing, and amenities because prospects will ask specific questions and vague answers lose appointments. Cortland uses OneSite for property management operations, and familiarity with L2L (Lead to Lease) is a plus. Bilingual fluency is welcome given the diverse renter base across Cortland's markets.

What Cortland Is Looking For

  • At least 2 years of customer service experience, with a preference for direct sales backgrounds
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills across phone, email, and text
  • Comfort with high outbound contact volume daily
  • Proficiency in OneSite; L2L experience preferred
  • English fluency required; bilingual candidates encouraged

Cortland is a vertically integrated multifamily operator founded in 2005, ranked among the top 10 multifamily owners in the US. They handle design, construction, and management in-house, which gives their leasing teams more product knowledge to work with than you'd find at a typical third-party management shop. The Sandy Springs, GA location puts you close to Cortland's Atlanta headquarters.

For someone building a career in multifamily, this role develops skills that transfer directly into leasing management, regional sales support, or corporate training. The exposure to lead management systems and portfolio-wide traffic patterns gives you a broader view of operations than a single-property leasing role typically allows.

job.title: Leasing & Sales Consultant - Cortland Vesta
job.description:
Leasing consultants at Cortland aren't just showing floor plans and handing out applications. The expectation is closer to a sales rep with a pipeline to manage, conversion targets to hit, and a brand standard to uphold on every single tour. At Cortland Vesta in Birmingham, that means you're the first and most important impression a prospective resident gets of the community.

What the Role Actually Requires

Occupancy doesn't manage itself. You'll own the full leasing cycle: generating and nurturing leads, running tours, handling objections, and closing leases. CRM discipline matters here. Cortland tracks lead activity, conversion metrics, and pipeline health, so your follow-up habits show up in the numbers. A prospect who toured on Tuesday and hasn't heard from you by Thursday is a missed opportunity reflected in your traffic-to-lease ratio.

The consultative side of the role is real, not just a buzzword. You're trying to understand what a prospect actually needs, a shorter commute, a quieter unit, a specific move-in date, and match them to the right home rather than pitching the same script to everyone. That approach tends to produce better close rates and stronger resident retention downstream.

Beyond leasing, you'll support community marketing and social media presence and help plan resident events that keep occupancy sticky. Renewals start with the experience you create before someone even signs their first lease.

What Separates Strong Candidates Here

Cortland operates as a vertically integrated platform with in-house construction, design, and management. That means the product is consistent and the brand standard is specific. You're not selling a commodity. The candidates who do well in this environment tend to be organized and coachable in equal measure. Organized because pipeline management falls apart without disciplined follow-up. Coachable because Cortland tracks KPIs closely and will give you direct feedback on what's working and what isn't.

  • 6 or more months of sales, customer service, or performance-driven experience preferred
  • Comfortable being measured against leasing goals and conversion targets
  • CRM experience or a clear ability to learn and use one consistently
  • Strong follow-up habits and time management
  • Professional presentation that matches a Class A resident experience
  • Ability to handle objections without getting rattled

Where This Role Can Go

Leasing consultant positions at well-capitalized operators like Cortland are a legitimate path into assistant property management and eventually community management. The skills you build here, pipeline management, resident relationship-building, sales under pressure, transfer cleanly into NOI-focused management roles. Birmingham's multifamily market has seen steady development activity, which means there's room to grow within the portfolio if you perform.

Cortland was founded in 2005 and is recognized as one of the top 10 multifamily owners and operators in the country. It's an equal opportunity employer and participates in E-Verify.

job.title: Maintenance Technician-Cortland Wren Northlake
job.description:
Cortland sits among the top ten multifamily owners and operators in the country, and their maintenance teams carry real weight in how that reputation holds up at the property level. This Maintenance Technician role at Cortland Wren Northlake in Charlotte puts you at the center of day-to-day operations for a well-resourced organization that builds, owns, and manages its own communities.

What the Work Actually Looks Like

Your day moves fast and rarely follows a script. You might start the morning pulling HVAC preventative maintenance checks, shift to a plumbing diagnosis in a ground-floor unit by midday, and finish the afternoon closing out a backlog of work orders before an on-call rotation kicks in. The variety is real. Strong technicians here aren't just wrench-turners; they're the people who catch a failing gate latch or a flickering corridor light before it becomes a liability issue.

Core responsibilities include:

  • Diagnosing and repairing electrical, plumbing, HVAC, appliance, flooring, and general building system issues
  • Identifying and correcting unsafe conditions quickly, including broken lighting, damaged entry points, and structural hazards
  • Running scheduled preventative maintenance on HVAC units and building equipment
  • Monitoring building and security systems and reporting on their condition
  • Keeping parts and supply inventory stocked to avoid delays on work orders
  • Participating in on-call rotations and responding to after-hours emergencies
  • Documenting completed work and flagging recurring issues to the supervisor

Resident interactions happen constantly in this role. How you handle those moments, whether it's a quick hallway update or a tense conversation about a delayed repair, shapes how residents feel about the community overall. That's not a soft skill. It's a core part of the job.

What You'll Bring

  • EPA certification (Type I, II, or Universal) required
  • High school diploma or equivalent
  • 1 to 3 or more years in maintenance, facilities, or multifamily housing
  • Solid technical skills across electrical, plumbing, HVAC, and appliance repair
  • Ability to manage multiple work orders and shift priorities without dropping the ball
  • Comfort with physical work in varying conditions, including lifting, ladder work, and outdoor tasks
  • Willingness to participate in on-call schedules

Where This Role Can Take You

Maintenance Technician is one of the clearest career-building positions in multifamily. Technicians who develop strong HVAC and electrical depth, learn to lead make-ready turns efficiently, and build a track record of low delinquency on work orders are the candidates properties fight over for Lead Technician and Service Manager roles. At a vertically integrated company like Cortland, where construction and operations sit under the same roof, there's also exposure to capital improvement projects and renovation work that most third-party management shops simply don't offer. The skills you build here, technical range, resident communication, preventative maintenance discipline, compound across every future property you work on.

Charlotte's multifamily market has stayed active, and Cortland has a visible footprint in the region. For a technician looking to grow inside a structured, well-resourced organization rather than bounce between smaller operators, this is a practical step in the right direction.

job.title: Senior Leasing Hub Associate - Sandy Springs, GA
job.description:
This is a centralized, hub-based leasing role. You're not sitting at a property front desk greeting walk-ins. You're working remotely or from a central office, handling a high volume of inbound and outbound leads across multiple Cortland communities in the market.

What the Work Actually Looks Like

Your day runs on lead volume. Expect to manage 40 to 50 leads daily through RealPage's OneSite platform, working calls, emails, and texts from prospects who are at various stages of their apartment search. Your job is to qualify those leads, match each prospect to the right Cortland community based on their needs, and hand off warm, appointment-ready prospects to the onsite leasing teams.

You need to know Cortland's communities well. Pricing, floor plans, amenities, and local positioning. Prospects will ask detailed questions, and you can't afford to stumble. Cortland provides training and site visits to build that knowledge, but you have to put in the work to retain it.

This is a sales role. Outbound activity is a real expectation, not an afterthought. If you're more comfortable waiting for inbound traffic than initiating contact, this isn't the right fit.

What You Bring

  • At least two years in customer service, with a clear sales component
  • Strong written and verbal communication skills, bilingual candidates welcome
  • Comfort with OneSite (RealPage); familiarity with L2L is a plus
  • Fast, accurate typing and solid computer skills overall
  • Ability to sustain energy and quality across a high volume of daily contacts

Where This Role Can Take You

Hub leasing roles are relatively new in multifamily, and associates who excel here build a skill set that translates well. You'll develop deep knowledge of how leads move through a leasing funnel, how to handle objections at scale, and how to coordinate across multiple properties simultaneously. That cross-community exposure is something most traditional leasing consultants don't get. Strong performers here often move into senior leasing, leasing manager, or training roles within larger operators like Cortland.

Cortland is a top-10 multifamily owner and operator in the US, vertically integrated with in-house design and construction. The organization is large enough to offer real career movement, and this role puts you in front of the leasing function at the portfolio level from day one.

job.title: Service Manager - Cortland Sun River
job.description:
Cortland runs a vertically integrated operation, which means a Service Manager here carries more weight than the same title at a typical third-party managed community. You're not just keeping the lights on. You're protecting an asset that Cortland owns, designs, and operates under one roof, and your team's performance feeds directly into resident retention, NOI, and the long-term value of the property.

What This Role Actually Involves

The core of this position is maintenance leadership at a multifamily community. You'll diagnose and resolve complex issues across HVAC, plumbing, electrical, appliances, and structural systems, and you'll make sure your technicians can do the same. That means hands-on training, daily task assignment, work order oversight, and stepping in yourself when the situation calls for it. Make-readies, punch list inspections, preventive maintenance scheduling, and on-call coverage all fall within your scope.

Beyond the technical side, you're managing the full maintenance operation: vendor coordination, parts and supply inventory, budget tracking, and safety compliance. Cortland expects you to stay ahead of problems rather than react to them. If your team is consistently catching issues early, before a resident submits a request, you're doing the job right.

What Cortland Needs From You

  • 3 to 5 or more years of maintenance experience covering carpentry, plumbing, electrical, and HVAC. Multifamily background is strongly preferred.
  • Universal EPA certification and CPO certification, both required at hire.
  • Valid Class E driver's license.
  • Working knowledge of OSHA, ADA, EPA regulations, and Fair Housing requirements.
  • Demonstrated ability to lead, train, and mentor a maintenance team.
  • Comfort managing a high volume of details: open work orders, vendor schedules, budget line items, and preventive maintenance records simultaneously.
  • Availability for evenings, weekends, and on-call rotation.
  • Physical ability to lift, climb, and work in both indoor and outdoor conditions.

What Sets Strong Candidates Apart

A lot of applicants can show technical competence. Fewer can show they've built a team culture where technicians take ownership and flag problems proactively without being asked. At Cortland's scale, reactive maintenance is expensive. The Service Managers who advance here are the ones who run tight preventive maintenance programs, keep delinquent work orders from piling up, and communicate clearly with both their team and residents. If you've managed a maintenance crew through a heavy capex project or a lease-up push, that experience translates directly.

Cortland was founded in 2005 and has grown into one of the ten largest multifamily owners and operators in the country. The in-house structure means decisions move faster and resources are generally more accessible than in a typical fee management setup. For a Service Manager who wants visibility and room to grow into a regional or director-level maintenance role, that structure matters.

job.title: Service Technician - Cortland Aertson
job.description:
Multifamily operators at scale run into a specific staffing challenge: they need technicians who can handle the full range of day-to-day maintenance competently, but who also fit into a structured, systems-driven operation. Cortland, one of the ten largest apartment owners and operators in the country, built its maintenance program around that reality. This role at Cortland Aertson in Nashville puts you inside that system.

What the Work Actually Looks Like

Your day moves between scheduled work orders and whatever the property surfaces. That means diagnosing and repairing electrical, plumbing, HVAC, appliances, flooring, and fixtures, then correcting unsafe conditions before they become liability issues. You're also responsible for keeping preventive maintenance on schedule, which matters more than most technicians realize. Equipment that gets skipped on PM cycles fails faster, and emergency repairs always cost more than preventive ones.

Security systems, automatic doors, and surveillance cameras fall under your watch too. You'll manage your own parts inventory to keep work order completion times tight. On-call rotations are part of the role, so resident and property emergencies after hours are a real expectation, not a footnote.

  • EPA certification required (Cortland offers in-house certified proctors if you need CPO or CPR)
  • 1 to 3 years of experience in facilities or multifamily maintenance
  • Hands-on competency across electrical, plumbing, HVAC, and general repairs
  • Comfortable identifying and correcting unsafe conditions independently
  • Able to manage parts inventory and coordinate with on-site staff on work completion

Why This Role Has Real Upside

Nashville's multifamily market has seen significant lease-up activity over the last several years, and that growth brought a wave of Class A properties that now need experienced maintenance staff to keep them performing. Working at a Cortland asset gives you exposure to a vertically integrated operation where design, construction, and property management sit under the same roof. That structure means decisions move faster, resources are more accessible, and the training programs are built in-house rather than outsourced.

Cortland offers a personalized growth plan alongside mentorship and leadership support, which is worth taking seriously if you're thinking about where this role leads. Technicians who build their certification portfolio and develop strong diagnostic skills typically move into lead or supervisor roles within a few years. Monthly commission is included in the compensation package alongside full benefits.

The candidates who stand out here aren't just technically solid. They communicate well with residents, close work orders without being chased, and treat preventive maintenance as seriously as emergency calls. If you approach the job that way, the path forward inside a company this size is straightforward.

job.title: Regional Manager, Affordable Housing
job.description:
The Skills That Drive This Role

Regional management in affordable housing sits at the intersection of regulatory precision, people leadership, and financial oversight. These aren't parallel tracks. They run together every single day. If you're someone who can hold a LIHTC compliance review in the morning, coach a struggling community manager through a performance conversation at noon, and then dig into a variance report before the day ends, this role was built around your skill set.

Asset Living is a third-party management firm ranked in the NMHC top 50, operating across multifamily, student, build-to-rent, and affordable housing portfolios nationwide. This Regional Manager position covers a portfolio of affordable communities in the Arlington, WA area, and the work is genuinely specialized. Affordable housing regional management isn't just conventional property oversight with a compliance checkbox added. The regulatory layer is substantial, and it shapes everything from how your teams process applications to how you interact with funding agencies and ownership groups.

What You'll Actually Be Managing

On the compliance side, you'll be responsible for ensuring your sites follow all LIHTC and Section 8 policies correctly. That means monitoring annual recertification processes, conducting file reviews, performing semi-annual physical inspections, and maintaining relationships with regulatory agencies. Fair housing and Equal Housing Opportunity requirements aren't background noise here. They're front and center.

Personnel management takes up a real portion of your time. You'll screen, hire, train, and develop on-site staff across your portfolio. You'll also handle the harder parts: performance documentation, terminations when necessary, salary review approvals, and timesheet oversight. Asset Living expects its regional leaders to handle these situations with consistency and clear communication up the chain to senior leadership and HR.

Financially, you're accountable for NOI across the portfolio. That means building annual operating budgets, monitoring income and expense variances, flagging trends, and making capital improvement recommendations. You'll review vendor contracts, approve invoices, and ensure month-end close and ownership reporting happen accurately and on time.

Leasing strategy is part of the picture too, including developing marketing plans, ensuring staff leasing techniques are effective, and building resident retention programs that reduce turnover. Every unnecessary turn costs money and disrupts residents who often have limited housing options. That context matters in affordable.

What Separates Strong Candidates Here

Affordable housing compliance knowledge is the real differentiator. Candidates who've managed conventional portfolios but have no LIHTC or Section 8 experience face a steep learning curve in this seat. Regulatory agencies don't grade on a curve, and neither do audits. If you've already worked through file reviews, corrective action plans from agencies, or managed a lease-up under affordable program requirements, that background is genuinely valuable here.

Beyond compliance, the strongest regional managers in affordable housing tend to be people who lead with clarity rather than authority. Your on-site teams deal with complex resident situations, income verification stress, and documentation demands that go well beyond what conventional teams typically handle. They need a regional who sets clear expectations, provides real coaching, and doesn't disappear when things get hard.

  • Demonstrated LIHTC and/or Section 8 compliance experience
  • Regional or multi-site property management background
  • Strong financial acumen including budget development and NOI management
  • Experience hiring, coaching, and managing on-site property staff
  • Familiarity with regulatory agency relationships and reporting
  • Solid understanding of fair housing law

Asset Living's AMO accreditation through IREM signals a commitment to professional standards that tends to attract operators who take the craft seriously. If you want a portfolio where the work is substantive and the compliance stakes are real, this is worth a closer look.

job.title: Lead Maintenance Technician - Riata
job.description:
The Technical Range This Role Demands

Lead Maintenance Technician at Riata isn't a one-trade job. You're moving across HVAC, plumbing, electrical, carpentry, and mechanical repairs on any given day, and the depth of that range matters. A technician who's strong in one area but thin in the others will feel it fast. The residents at this Austin community notice response times, and the team you're leading will take cues from how you handle the tough calls: the compressor that fails on a Friday afternoon, the water heater that goes out over a holiday weekend.

Olympus Property has been recognized as a National Apartment Association Top Employer three years running (2023, 2024, 2025). That's not just a badge. It signals that the company is watching how people are treated at the team level, not just the resident level. For a lead tech, that matters because you're hiring, training, and evaluating the people under you. Your ability to develop the team directly affects how well the property runs.

What You're Actually Responsible For

This role carries supervisory weight alongside hands-on work. You're conducting routine inspections of the property's interior and exterior, coordinating exterior vendors like landscaping, managing proper trash removal procedures, and keeping the Community Compliance Manual current. You're also the person who makes sure tools, equipment, and materials are stored safely and that OSHA standards aren't just a poster on a wall.

  • Two to five years of multi-family maintenance experience, with prior supervisory time preferred
  • Working knowledge across HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and carpentry
  • HVAC, EPA, and/or CPO certifications may be required based on property needs
  • Comfort with operations technology platforms and basic computer communication
  • Flexibility for days, evenings, weekdays, and weekends
  • High school diploma or equivalent required

The compensation range sits between $80,000 and $90,000, with monthly and quarterly bonuses available. That's a real number for Austin's market and reflects the scope of what this role covers.

What Separates Strong Candidates Here

Austin's rental market runs hot. Properties in this city deal with high turn volume and residents who have options. A lead tech who treats make-readies as just a checklist will produce units that feel like it. The best candidates here think about the punch list differently: they know which items a prospective resident will notice during a tour and they sequence the work accordingly.

The supervisory piece is where many technicians hit a ceiling. Doing the work well and teaching others to do it well are different skills. If you've coached a junior tech through a difficult repair, run a team through a safety protocol, or held a performance conversation, those experiences translate directly to what Olympus is looking for here. The culture emphasis on teamwork and respect isn't just language. It shows up in how leads are expected to run their teams day to day.

job.title: Property Manager
job.description:
Weinstein Properties has been family-owned for over 70 years, and that history shows up in how they actually operate. This isn't a company where a Property Manager gets a key, a login, and a "figure it out." Corporate teams stay close, processes are clearly defined, and the expectation is that you'll run your community with both independence and accountability. That combination is harder to find than it sounds.

The open role is at Bexley Duluth, a Class A apartment community in Duluth, GA. Weinstein manages more than 60 communities across Virginia, North Carolina, Texas, Tennessee, and Georgia, so the infrastructure behind this position is real. You're not operating in isolation.

What the role actually demands day to day: you're the first person residents reach when something has escalated beyond the leasing staff's ability to resolve it. You're coordinating with your Maintenance Supervisor on project timelines and community standards. You're reviewing your key performance metrics, managing delinquency, watching occupancy trends, and making sure your team has the direction they need to perform. The administrative side is genuine. So is the people side. You'll need to hold both at once without letting either slip.

This role builds something specific. Property Managers who come through highly structured, operations-driven companies tend to develop a sharper understanding of process design and accountability systems than those who work in more autonomous environments. That translates directly if you ever move into a regional or multi-site role, because you'll already know how to think about consistency at scale, not just execution at a single property.

What separates strong candidates here from average ones isn't just experience. It's the ability to work within a defined structure without feeling constrained by it. Weinstein's model is high-touch by design. Managers who chafe under frequent corporate communication or who prefer to build their own systems from scratch will likely find this environment frustrating. Managers who appreciate clear expectations and see structured support as an asset rather than a leash will thrive.

What you'll need to be considered

  • Prior property management experience, required
  • Class A multifamily background, strongly preferred
  • Demonstrated ability to lead and develop on-site teams
  • Strong communication skills and a genuine sense of urgency
  • Comfort managing high request volume from both residents and corporate simultaneously
  • Detail-oriented, organized, and solutions-focused by default

Compensation is competitive and tied to experience. Quarterly and annual bonus opportunities are part of the package. If you've put in the work to understand how a well-run apartment community actually operates, and you want to do that work inside a company that will back you up with real support, this is worth a close look.

job.title: Leasing Consultant
job.description:
Student housing runs on a different clock than conventional multifamily. Lease-up seasons are intense, turn periods are compressed and chaotic, and the residents you're serving are often renting their first apartment without a parent holding their hand. The Experience Team Member role at The Republic at Lubbock sits right in the middle of all that energy, and it's genuinely a different animal than a standard leasing consultant position at a garden-style or Class A conventional community.

Scion is one of the larger operators in the student housing space, and this property serves the Texas Tech market. The ETM role blends leasing, marketing, resident engagement, and light property administration into a single position. That's not a criticism. It's just the reality of how student housing communities tend to staff their front-line teams. You'll be giving tours, handling package retrieval, jumping on social media to push engagement, helping plan resident events, and occasionally covering after-hours lockout calls. Some days you'll do all of that before lunch.

What makes someone genuinely good at this role isn't just friendliness, though that matters. It's the ability to shift gears without losing your footing. A prospective resident and their parent walk in right as you're logging packages and a maintenance request just came through the queue. You handle all three without any of them feeling like an afterthought. That composure under layered demands is what separates strong ETMs from ones who burn out by November.

The on-call rotation is worth taking seriously before you apply. After-hours lockout duty is part of the job, not an occasional exception. If that's a dealbreaker, it's better to know now than to find out mid-semester.

What Scion is looking for

  • A genuine customer-first mindset, not just a friendly face at the front desk
  • Strong communication skills across a diverse resident population, including international students
  • Flexibility to work weekends, holidays, and through turn periods when the property flips hundreds of units in a matter of days
  • Self-discipline and reliability, because this role involves a lot of independent judgment in real time
  • Comfort with social media and digital communication as actual leasing and engagement tools

For someone early in a property management career, this role builds a real foundation. The combination of leasing, marketing, and operations exposure you get in student housing is hard to replicate elsewhere. ETMs who develop their sales instincts and learn to read traffic patterns and objections often move into assistant manager or leasing director tracks faster than their peers at conventional properties. The pace of student housing accelerates that learning curve considerably.

This is a part-time position based on-site at The Republic at Lubbock. Work authorization in the United States is required. Visa sponsorship is not available.

job.title: Leasing Consultant
job.description:
Student housing runs on energy, consistency, and genuine connection. This leasing consultant role at Redpoint Wilmington puts all three of those skills to work every single day. The Scion Group calls this position an Experience Team Member, and that title is more accurate than "leasing consultant" in a meaningful way: closing a lease is only part of the job.

Skills This Role Requires on Day One

You'll need real communication ability before you walk in the door. Not just friendliness, but the capacity to read a prospective resident's hesitation, understand the objection underneath it, and respond without sounding scripted. Student housing prospects often bring parents into the conversation, which means you're sometimes selling to two different audiences at once. You'll also need to stay organized under pressure. Package logging, maintenance follow-ups, office opening and closing procedures, and social media engagement can all land on your plate in the same afternoon shift.

  • Comfortable giving community and amenity tours to prospects with varied expectations
  • Able to document and communicate sales objections clearly to management
  • Familiar with social media platforms and capable of engaging authentically, not just posting
  • Available during Turn periods, summers, and holidays, which are the highest-traffic seasons in student housing
  • Willing to participate in after-hours on-call rotation for lockout situations

Skills This Role Builds Over Time

If you're early in a property management career, this position is one of the better training grounds available. Student housing lease-ups are fast and high-volume. You'll learn how to manage traffic, track objections, and contribute to local marketing strategy in ways that a conventional multifamily leasing role might not expose you to as quickly. The planning and execution of resident engagement events builds event coordination skills that carry into assistant manager and community manager roles later. Working inside a structured system like Scion's also gives you exposure to how national operators standardize culture and process across a large portfolio, which is a transferable skill regardless of where your career goes next.

Student housing has its own rhythm. The academic calendar drives nearly everything: move-in, move-out, occupancy pressure, and renewal urgency all cluster around predictable windows. Understanding that rhythm early in your career gives you a framework for thinking about leasing strategy that's genuinely useful across property types.

What Makes Strong Candidates Stand Out Here

The candidates who thrive in this role aren't necessarily the most polished. They're the ones who stay consistent when the environment shifts quickly, who follow up without being reminded, and who treat every tour like it matters even on a slow Tuesday.
